externalTools.title: External Coding Tools
externalTools.description: Used by CountBot to call external coding tools.
externalTools.helpQuickStartStep1: Create a profile on this page and fill in the name, command, arguments, working directory, session mode, and timeout.
externalTools.helpQuickStartStep2: After saving, CountBot writes the configuration to external_coding_tools.json in the workspace. All later calls use that file.
externalTools.helpQuickStartStep3: If you want an IM channel to use a tool by default, also go to IM Channels -> target account -> Advanced and set the default route plus default coding tool there.
externalTools.helpQuickStartStep4: Once configured, you can temporarily pick a tool in chat or let the default tool handle the current session automatically.
externalTools.helpProfilesBody: Configure external coding tools like Claude, Codex, and OpenCode here. Once saved, CountBot can call them from chat for coding tasks.
externalTools.helpImStep1: First enable and save the target account under IM Channels. Tool profiles alone do not make chat traffic use them.
externalTools.helpImStep2: In AI mode, CountBot handles the message first and only calls the external coding tool when it is needed.
externalTools.helpImStep3: In direct mode, the chat message is sent straight to the default coding tool. This is better for long-running coding sessions.
externalTools.helpImStep4: Commands like /coder, /cdr, /route, and /rt only affect the current chat session. Saved values in Advanced are the defaults.
externalTools.helpCommandsBody: There are two common ways to use tools: explicitly mention one in natural language, or switch the current session route with commands.
externalTools.helpModesBody: In AI mode, CountBot handles the message first and calls external tools only when needed. In direct mode, the raw user message is sent straight to the default coding tool, which is better for continuous coding sessions.
externalTools.helpModesStep1: Single Turn: only sends the current request. Cleanest, but with the least context.
externalTools.helpModesStep2: Recent History: includes the latest N messages. This is the safest default for most coding chats.
externalTools.helpModesStep3: Native Session: prefers the tool's own session mechanism. It currently fits tools like Claude better.
externalTools.helpRoutingBody: Default route mode and default coding tool are configured under Advanced in each IM channel account and saved to backend config. /route, /rt, /coder, and /cdr are sent inside the IM chat window, only affect the current chat, and are not persisted.
externalTools.helpFieldsStep1: Command: use either an absolute path or a command name available in PATH. If you use a plain name, the backend runtime must be able to resolve it.
externalTools.helpFieldsStep2: Arguments: one argument per line. Template variables like prompt and working_dir are replaced when the tool starts.
externalTools.helpFieldsStep3: Working Directory: usually point this to the project root or a specific subdirectory so the tool runs in the right place.
externalTools.helpFieldsStep4: Extra Environment Variables: inject API keys or runtime flags into the child process. Inherited Environment Variables controls which existing env vars are passed through.
externalTools.empty: No profiles yet. Use the button above to create one.
externalTools.profileHint: Configure how one external coding CLI should be launched.
externalTools.enableHint: CountBot can only call this tool in chat when it is enabled.
externalTools.historyModeHint: History mode sends the latest N chat messages together with the current request. It is the safest choice for continuous coding chats.
externalTools.nativeModeHint: Native session mode prefers the tool's own session mechanism. It currently works best with Claude and will fall back to recent history for unsupported tools.
externalTools.commandHint: Use either an absolute path or a command name available in PATH.
externalTools.workingDirHint: Default working directory relative to the workspace. Leave empty to run from the workspace root.
externalTools.argsHint: One argument per line. Template variables such as prompt and working_dir are supported.
externalTools.envHint: JSON object that will be injected into the child process environment.
externalTools.routeHint: Default route and default coding tool are configured under Advanced in each IM channel account.
externalTools.svgHint: Optional raw SVG markup. It is shown in the profile tabs and preview card.

providers.title: Model Provider
providers.apiKeysHint: Add multiple API keys. The backend will rotate automatically when rate limits or quota exhaustion are detected.

notes.native: This provider has a dedicated request mapping and can explicitly ask the upstream model to enable or disable thinking.
notes.limited: This provider can only lower reasoning effort where supported; it may not fully disable internal reasoning.
notes.response_only: This provider mainly controls whether reasoning is returned in the response, not whether the model reasons internally.
notes.experimental: This provider relies on an upstream compatibility layer or model-specific behavior, so the switch may be ignored.
notes.compat: This provider only passes compatible fields through. Effectiveness depends on your upstream gateway, model, or local server.
notes.unsupported: This provider does not currently have a reliable thinking-control mapping, so you should not depend on the switch.
notes.anthropic: Anthropic uses native thinking configuration, but the parameter is only sent when the output token budget is large enough.
notes.openai: OpenAI maps to reasoning_effort. GPT-5 family models will request lower or disabled reasoning when possible, but supported levels vary by model.
notes.openrouter: OpenRouter maps to its reasoning compatibility fields, but whether thinking is truly disabled depends on the routed upstream model and provider.
notes.deepseek: DeepSeek maps to thinking.type and can switch between reasoning and direct-response modes.
notes.moonshot: Moonshot/Kimi maps to thinking.type. Tool-enabled requests still prefer disabling thinking to preserve argument budget.
notes.zhipu: Zhipu GLM maps to thinking.type and can explicitly request reasoning on or off.
notes.groq: Groq currently prioritizes include_reasoning. It can usually hide reasoning output, but not every model can truly stop internal reasoning.
notes.qwen: Alibaba Bailian maps to enable_thinking and can explicitly request reasoning on or off.
notes.minimax: MiniMax currently enters through an Anthropic-compatible layer, where thinking behavior may be ignored or unstable.

model.title: Model Parameters
model.temperatureDesc: Controls randomness. Set to 0 to omit this parameter and use the provider default; higher values make output more random, lower values more deterministic.
model.maxTokensDesc: Caps response output length, not the full context window. Set to 0 to omit this parameter; the model or gateway will usually decide it.
model.maxIterationsDesc: Maximum number of tool calls the agent can make to prevent infinite loops.
model.thinkingEnabledDesc: When disabled, CountBot will prefer a direct answer. On supported models or gateways, this usually reduces time-to-first-token. If reasoning still comes back, CountBot will keep showing it, which means the upstream side did not actually disable reasoning.

skills.title: Skills Library
skills.description: Manage and enable AI skills to extend assistant capabilities
skills.noSkillsDesc: No skill files found in the skills directory
skills.searchPlaceholder: Search by skill name or description
skills.noSearchResultsDesc: No skills match the current keyword or filter selection. Try another query or reset the filters.
skills.noFilterResultsDesc: No skills are available under the current status filter. Switch filters or reset to view all skills.
skills.autoLoadTooltip: This skill will be automatically loaded on startup
skills.countbotGuideTitle: How to Install Skills in CountBot
skills.countbotGuideDescription: If you want the cleanest and most stable workflow, use CountBot's own installation flow first. You can create a skill directly in the UI, or copy a downloaded skill directory into the current workspace skills folder for local management.
skills.countbotHeroBadgeBoundary: Not a full official ecosystem bridge
skills.countbotSpecDesc: CountBot borrows and supports the directory-based skill shape used by anthropics/skills, while focusing on stable local workspace management.
skills.countbotSpecPoint1: Skills are organized by directory, with SKILL.md used as the primary entry file.
skills.countbotSpecPoint2: Markdown skill content and structured frontmatter remain part of the supported shape.
skills.countbotSpecPoint3: CountBot adds its own metadata, configuration, and import workflow on top of that compatible base.
skills.countbotCompatibilityTitle: Does CountBot truly implement the spec integration?
skills.countbotCompatibilityDesc: Yes, but as foundational compatibility plus local adaptation rather than a full reproduction of the anthropics/skills repository ecosystem or installation flow.
skills.countbotCompatibilityVerdict: Verdict: CountBot does implement baseline compatibility with the SKILL.md directory model and supports discovery/import from OpenClaw-compatible directories, but it does not fully implement an official marketplace, online install flow, publishing pipeline, or product-level ecosystem bridge.
skills.countbotImplementedPoint1: Discover and load skill directories that use SKILL.md as the anchor file.
skills.countbotImplementedPoint2: Create, enable, disable, edit, and configure skills directly inside CountBot.
skills.countbotImplementedPoint3: Scan OpenClaw-compatible directories and import a discovered skill into the current workspace on first enable.
skills.countbotNotImplementedPoint1: No direct marketplace-style integration or online search/install flow for anthropics/skills.
skills.countbotNotImplementedPoint2: No account-level sync, hosting, or managed distribution with Anthropic-side products or services.
skills.countbotNotImplementedPoint3: No full reproduction of the broader upstream ecosystem around repository operations, publishing, or official integrations.
skills.countbotInstallDesc: All three methods land in the current workspace and are better suited for long-term editing, configuration, and maintenance.
skills.countbotMethod1Title: Create a skill directly in the page
skills.countbotMethod1Desc: Best when you want to write a skill from scratch or add a new one quickly. The skill is stored in the current workspace immediately, making later editing and management straightforward.
skills.countbotMethod2Title: Copy a downloaded skill into the workspace manually
skills.countbotMethod2Desc: Useful when you already have a ready-made skill directory. As long as you place the full folder under the current workspace skills directory, CountBot can discover it after refresh.
skills.countbotMethod3Title: Search and install online with find-skills
skills.countbotMethod3Desc: Use this when you want to discover an external skill first and then install it straight into the current workspace skills directory. The flow is adapted from the public APIs and download endpoints behind https://skillhub.tencent.com/.
skills.countbotMethod3Hint: Recommended flow: search first, then install. After installation, refresh this page to continue enabling, configuring, or editing the skill. For CLI bootstrap or command-line management, use bootstrap, list, enable, disable, or delete.
skills.countbotStructureDesc: Whether the skill is created from the page or copied manually, it is best to keep everything under the current workspace skills directory:
skills.countbotStructureHint: It is recommended that each skill directory contains SKILL.md so CountBot can identify and maintain it correctly.
skills.countbotWorkflowStep1: Decide whether you are creating a new skill or bringing an existing one into CountBot.
skills.countbotWorkflowStep2: For a new skill, use the Create Skill button. For an existing skill, copy the folder into workspace/skills/skill-name/.
skills.countbotWorkflowStep3: Make sure SKILL.md exists in the folder, then return to the skills page and refresh.
skills.countbotWorkflowStep4: Once discovered, the skill can be enabled, disabled, edited, configured, and managed entirely inside CountBot.
skills.countbotBestPracticeTitle: Why CountBot-local installation is recommended first
skills.countbotBestPracticePoint1: The runtime path and management path are the same, which makes issues easier to locate and avoids dependence on external folders.
skills.countbotBestPracticePoint2: Local skills can be edited, deleted, and configured directly, which is better for long-term maintenance.
skills.countbotBestPracticePoint3: If you still want OpenClaw ecosystem compatibility, you can discover external skills first and then import them into the workspace.
skills.countbotTipsPoint1: Keep skill names stable when possible to avoid duplicate-looking entries or folder confusion.
skills.countbotTipsPoint2: If you copied a skill from an external source, verify the directory structure before importing so a normal folder is not mistaken for a skill.
skills.countbotTipsPoint3: For skills you plan to keep enabled, storing them in the workspace skills directory is usually more stable and easier to back up.
skills.openclawGuideDescription: OpenClaw / Clawhub is a CLI ecosystem for AI skill extensions, but its official services are currently affected by broken APIs, server errors, and rate limits. This page covers the theoretical standard flow, practical troubleshooting, and the more stable way to use the same skills in CountBot.
skills.openclawImportHint: This skill comes from an external OpenClaw-compatible directory. The first enable action will copy it into the current workspace skills directory before enabling it.
skills.openclawDetailHint: OpenClaw source skills are currently read-only discoveries: you can inspect them and import-enable them, but you cannot edit or delete the external source directly from CountBot.
skills.openclawPrepareDesc: Windows, macOS, and Linux are supported. The examples below use Windows. Before anything else, make sure Node.js and npm are available in your shell.
skills.openclawNpmFixDesc: If an old or malformed .npmrc exists, clawhub-related commands may print mirror configuration warnings. Clearing the old file and resetting the registry first usually avoids that problem.
skills.openclawNpmFixStep3Label: Verify the new registry is active
skills.openclawStandardDesc: The following keeps the standard OpenClaw / Clawhub workflow for reference. If your real goal is stable usage inside CountBot, it is better to place skills into a CountBot-compatible directory and then use Import & Enable from the skills page.
skills.openclawWebTitle: Search and download skills from clawhub.ai
skills.openclawWebStep1: Open https://clawhub.ai and sign in. If the site does not open, the official service is likely unavailable.
skills.openclawWebStep2: Search the target skill on the Skills page, such as summarize or tavily-search.
skills.openclawWebStep3: After download, the official theoretical location is .clawhub/skills. Create the folder manually first if it does not exist.
skills.openclawWebStep4: If you want stable usage in CountBot, move or copy the downloaded skill into ~/.openclaw/skills/skill-name/ or ~/skills/skill-name/ afterward.
skills.openclawWebNote: Note: official Clawhub materials often use .clawhub/skills, while CountBot currently scans ~/.openclaw/skills and ~/skills. For CountBot, the compatible directories should be treated as the final source of truth.
skills.openclawCliTitle: Install / search skills through CLI commands
skills.openclawCliDesc: If Node.js is already installed, you can either install clawhub globally or use npx to call the latest version temporarily.
skills.guideInstallDesc: You can keep using the existing OpenClaw directory layout or place skills in a simpler home-directory compatibility path. CountBot scans and de-duplicates both, then imports the skill into the current workspace only when you enable it.
skills.guideInstallMethod1Desc: Best when you want to keep using skills already installed or organized for OpenClaw / clawhub. On macOS and Linux, this is the higher-priority compatible location.
skills.guideInstallMethod2Desc: Useful for manually collected, migrated, or temporarily organized skills. The shorter path also makes personal skill management easier.
skills.guideInstallMethod3Desc: On Windows, CountBot also prioritizes %USERPROFILE%\.openclaw\skills, which commonly resolves to the following absolute path.
skills.guideCompatibilityDesc: CountBot follows a professional, stable, and minimal strategy for OpenClaw compatibility:
skills.guideCompatibilityPoint1: It scans OpenClaw-compatible directories and identifies skills using SKILL.md as the anchor file.
skills.guideCompatibilityPoint2: External skills are shown as disabled by default so runtime behavior never depends on home-directory or external-path execution.
skills.guideCompatibilityPoint3: After you enable a discovered skill, CountBot copies it into the current workspace and runs it as a local CountBot skill.
skills.guideStructureDesc: Each skill directory must use SKILL.md as the discovery anchor. This minimum structure is recommended:
skills.guideStructureHint: If SKILL.md is missing, CountBot skips that directory so normal folders are not misidentified as skills.
skills.guidePathsDesc: CountBot scans the following OpenClaw-compatible skill directories by default and de-duplicates them automatically:
skills.guidePathsHint: Each skill directory should ideally use the skill-name/SKILL.md structure. After changing external directories, click refresh so CountBot can scan them again.
skills.guidePriorityPoint1: Overall priority is: workspace skills > built-in skills > external OpenClaw skills.
skills.guidePriorityPoint2: Within external directories, ~/.openclaw/skills has higher priority than ~/skills. On Windows, %USERPROFILE%\.openclaw\skills is also supported.
skills.guidePriorityPoint3: If multiple skills share the same name, only the highest-priority version is kept, preventing duplicate entries and ambiguous runtime sources.
skills.guideWorkflowStep1: Place the OpenClaw skill directory into a supported scan path and make sure SKILL.md exists inside it.
skills.guideWorkflowStep2: Refresh the CountBot Skills Library and find the discovered skill whose source is marked as OpenClaw.
skills.guideWorkflowStep3: Click Import & Enable. CountBot will copy the full directory into the current workspace at skills/skill-name.
skills.guideWorkflowStep4: After import, the skill becomes a workspace skill and can then be enabled, disabled, edited, or configured under normal CountBot rules.
skills.openclawCommandSearch: Search skills by keyword, for example clawhub search calendar.
skills.openclawCommandInstall: Install a specific skill, for example clawhub install tavily-search.
skills.openclawCommandRun: Run an installed skill directly with arguments.
skills.openclawTroubleshootingDesc: Most real-world issues are caused by Clawhub service outages, rate limits, or local Node/npm environment problems. The guidance below is focused on practical outcomes rather than ideal theory.
skills.openclawError1Title: Error 1: Skill not found / API not found
skills.openclawError1Body: This usually means the skill name is misspelled or the official skill API is no longer available. Verify the name first; if it still fails, stop depending on the official search service and switch to manual download or an alternative tool.
skills.openclawError2Title: Error 2: InternalServerError / Your request couldn't be completed
skills.openclawError2Body: This usually means the Clawhub server is failing. There is rarely a meaningful local fix. The practical response is to stop relying on the official service and use manual directories, alternatives, or a CountBot-local workflow instead.
skills.openclawError3Body: This is server-side rate limiting. You can wait 15–30 minutes and try again or switch networks, but if it keeps happening the ecosystem is not stable enough and a local import workflow is the safer choice.
skills.openclawError4Title: Error 4: npm warning Unknown user config "//registry.npmmirror.com"
skills.openclawError4Body: The usual cause is a malformed .npmrc file. Run the npm config fix commands shown above, remove the old config, and then retry the related command.
skills.openclawError5Title: Error 5: clawhub is not recognized as a command
skills.openclawError5Body: This usually means clawhub is not installed globally, or Node.js is not correctly added to PATH. Reinstall Node.js with Add to PATH enabled, run npm install -g clawhub, or temporarily use npx to run the latest clawhub command instead.
skills.openclawAlternativeTitle: 6. Alternatives and the more stable path
skills.openclawAlternativeDesc: If the official ecosystem keeps failing, you do not need to block yourself on its server status. For CountBot users, the following options are usually more stable:
skills.openclawAlternativePoint1: Download or organize skill directories manually and place them into ~/.openclaw/skills, ~/skills, or directly into the current workspace skills directory.
skills.openclawAlternativePoint2: For common capabilities such as summarization or search, consider standalone CLI tools or SDKs instead of relying on the official Clawhub service.
skills.openclawAlternativePoint3: If CountBot has already discovered an external skill, prefer Import & Enable so the runtime source becomes a workspace copy and is less affected by external paths or service instability.
skills.guideTroubleshootingPoint1: If clawhub services, mirrors, npm config, or PATH are unstable, you can still manually place the skill directory into a supported scan path and let CountBot discover/import it locally.
skills.guideTroubleshootingPoint2: If a skill does not appear after refresh, verify the directory structure and confirm that SKILL.md exists inside the skill folder.
skills.guideTroubleshootingPoint3: If an external skill is hidden by another one with the same name, that usually means the workspace or built-in version has higher priority to keep behavior stable and predictable.
skills.deleteConfirm: Are you sure you want to delete skill {name}? This action cannot be undone.
skills.cannotDeleteOpenclaw: Cannot delete external OpenClaw skills directly. Remove the external folder or the imported workspace copy instead
skills.skillNameHint: Skill name cannot be changed after creation
skills.skillDescriptionPlaceholder: Brief description of what this skill does
skills.contentPlaceholder:
Enter skill content here...

Markdown supported:
- **Bold** and *Italic*
- `Code` and code blocks
- Headings, lists, quotes, etc.

Skill content will be loaded and used by the AI assistant.
skills.autoLoadHint: Automatically load this skill on startup

cron.title: Scheduler
cron.description: Create and manage scheduled tasks for the AI assistant to execute automatically
cron.noJobsDesc: Create your first scheduled job to let the AI assistant work on a schedule
cron.scheduleHint: Use standard cron format: minute hour day month weekday
cron.messagePlaceholder: Enter the task description for the AI assistant...
cron.messageHint: This message will be sent to the AI assistant when triggered
cron.enabledHint: Disabled jobs won't run automatically but can be triggered manually
cron.deleteConfirm: Are you sure you want to delete job "{name}"?

errors.scheduleInvalid: Invalid cron expression format (should have 5 parts)

cron.deliverToChannelHint: When enabled, AI responses will be sent to the specified channel (Feishu, Telegram, etc.)
cron.accountIdPlaceholder: Use default for the primary bot, or bot / bot-2 for child bots
cron.accountIdHint: Used for multi-bot routing. It must match the configured account ID for channels such as Feishu or WeChat
cron.chatIdPlaceholder: Enter group ID or user ID
cron.chatIdHint: Feishu group ID, Telegram chat ID, etc.
cron.enableRetryHint: Automatically retry failed tasks to improve reliability
cron.deleteOnSuccessHint: Automatically delete task after successful execution, suitable for temporary reminders
cron.deleteOnSuccessWarning: Task will be automatically deleted after successful execution and cannot be recovered

tasks.title: Background Tasks
tasks.description: Monitor and manage running background tasks
tasks.noTasksDesc: No running or completed background tasks at the moment
tasks.noResult: This task did not return a result
tasks.deleteConfirm: Are you sure you want to delete task "{label}"?
